title: As the Dalai Lama Turns 91, India and China Are Fighting for the Future of Buddhism
gist: As the Dalai Lama turns 91, India and China are contesting custodianship of Buddhism itself, with the Dalai Lama affirming his Gaden Phodrang Trust holds sole authority over his reincarnation in the 'free world' while Beijing insists any successor comply with Chinese law and the Qing-era Golden Urn ritual.

insights.claim: The Dalai Lama's birthday has become an annual reminder of a ticking clock over who will choose the 15th Dalai Lama, behind which lies a broader India-China contest over custodianship of Buddhism itself.
insights.speaker: Kritee Chopra
insights.so_what: The succession is a strategic flashpoint that could sharpen India-China tensions on the Tibetan question.
insights.locator: But the birthday of the Dalai Lama is no longer

insights.claim: At his 90th birthday in July 2025, the Dalai Lama affirmed the institution will continue after his death and that his Gaden Phodrang Trust holds sole authority to recognize his reincarnation, pointedly excluding Beijing.
insights.speaker: Kritee Chopra
insights.so_what: Sets up a certain succession dispute with two competing Dalai Lamas, one India-based and one Beijing-endorsed.
insights.locator: At his 90th birthday celebrations in July 2025

insights.claim: China insists any Dalai Lama reincarnation must comply with Chinese law and the Qing-era Golden Urn ritual codified in its 2007 State Religious Affairs Bureau Order No. 5, which requires state approval for all reincarnations of living Buddhas.
insights.speaker: Kritee Chopra
insights.so_what: Beijing's pre-positioned legal framework signals it will install its own successor, guaranteeing a legitimacy clash.
insights.locator: Within hours, China rejected the plan
